content.observe: An OS didn't always look like this. It started as a way to avoid wasting a very expensive machine's idle time.
content.fault: You assumed the OS has always managed memory, scheduling, and users the way it does now. It hasn't — each of those was a response to a specific failure.

hotspots.label: Batch Systems
hotspots.detail: Jobs queued and run one after another with no interaction — the operator was the scheduler.

hotspots.label: Multiprogramming
hotspots.detail: Multiple jobs held in memory at once so the CPU always has something to run.

hotspots.label: Time-Sharing
hotspots.detail: The CPU rotates between users fast enough that each one feels like they have it alone.

hotspots.label: Modern OS
hotspots.detail: Manages processes, memory, storage, and protection as one integrated resource manager.
